sections.title: The Ergative ने — When and Why
sections.explanation:
Hindi uses a special grammatical system called 'ergative alignment' in the perfective aspect. When a transitive verb is in the perfective (completed action — simple past, present perfect, past perfect), the subject takes the postposition ने. This is a major structural difference from English.

THE RULE:
Transitive verb + perfective aspect → subject takes ने

WHAT CHANGES:
When ने is added to the subject, the verb no longer agrees with the subject. Instead, it agrees with the object in gender and number — but only if the object does not have a postposition (like को).

  उसने किताब पढ़ी। = He read a book.
  Here: उसने = he (with ने), किताब = book (feminine), पढ़ी = read (feminine singular, agreeing with किताब).

  Compare with the imperfective (no ने):
  वह किताब पढ़ता है। = He reads a book.
  Here: वह = he (no ने), पढ़ता = reads (masculine singular, agreeing with वह).

NON-ERGATIVE CASES:
ने is NOT used with:
  - Intransitive verbs: वह गया। (He went.) — no ने because जाना is intransitive.
  - Imperfective aspect: वह खाना खाता है। (He eats food.) — present habitual, not perfective.
  - Future tense: वह खाना खाएगा। (He will eat food.) — future, not perfective.
sections.tip: Ask yourself two questions: (1) Is the verb transitive? (2) Is the action completed (perfective)? If both answers are yes, the subject takes ने and the verb agrees with the object.

examples.en: He read a book. (verb agrees with किताब, feminine)
The girl ate food. (verb agrees with खाना, masculine)
We watched a movie. (verb agrees with फ़िल्म, feminine)
He went. (जाना is intransitive, so no ने is used)
Ram wrote a letter. (verb agrees with पत्र, masculine)

exercises.prompt: "उसने किताब पढ़ी" में क्रिया किससे मेल खाती है? (In this sentence, what does the verb agree with?)
options: Neither — the verb is always masculine
exercises.explanation: In ergative constructions with ने, the verb agrees with the object, not the subject. किताब is feminine, so the verb takes the feminine form पढ़ी instead of masculine पढ़ा.
ने is only used with transitive verbs in the perfective aspect. सोना (to sleep) is intransitive, so no ने. जाता है is imperfective, so no ने. खाना खाया is transitive + perfective, so ने is correct.

exercises.prompt: लड़की ___ खाना खाया। (The girl ate food.)
exercises.hint: Postposition used with transitive verbs in perfective
exercises.explanation: ने is required because खाना (to eat) is transitive and the action is perfective (completed). The verb खाया agrees with खाना (masculine).

sections.title: Verb Agreement in Ergative Sentences
sections.explanation:
When ने is used, verb agreement follows specific rules based on whether the object is marked with a postposition.

RULE 1 — Object without postposition:
The verb agrees with the object in gender and number.
  उसने रोटी खाई। = He ate bread. (रोटी is feminine → खाई)
  उसने समोसा खाया। = He ate a samosa. (समोसा is masculine → खाया)
  उसने रोटियाँ खाईं। = He ate rotis. (रोटियाँ is feminine plural → खाईं)

RULE 2 — Object with postposition (को, etc.):
When the object takes a postposition like को, the verb cannot agree with it. The verb defaults to masculine singular.
  उसने लड़के को देखा। = He saw the boy. (लड़के को has postposition → देखा, default masculine singular)
  उसने लड़की को देखा। = He saw the girl. (लड़की को has postposition → देखा, still default masculine singular)

RULE 3 — No object:
If there is no explicit object, the verb defaults to masculine singular.
  उसने खाया। = He ate. (no object → खाया, default)

The को postposition is often used with specific/definite or animate objects, which is why verb agreement patterns can shift within the same conversation.
sections.tip: If the object has को after it, the verb 'gives up' trying to agree and defaults to masculine singular. This is the most common source of errors for learners.

examples.en: He wrote a letter. (चिट्ठी feminine → लिखी)
He wrote a letter. (पत्र masculine → लिखा)
Mother called the children. (बच्चों को has postposition → बुलाया default)
He/She read all the books. (किताबें feminine plural → पढ़ीं)

exercises.prompt: "उसने लड़की को देखा" — क्रिया 'देखा' पुल्लिंग क्यों है? (Why is the verb masculine?)
options: Because the subject उसने is masculine
Because the object लड़की has the postposition को, so the verb defaults to masculine singular
exercises.explanation: When the object is marked with को, the verb cannot agree with it and defaults to masculine singular (देखा). Without को, it would be: उसने लड़की देखी (feminine, agreeing with लड़की).

exercises.hint: Past tense of खाना, agreeing with रोटी (feminine)
exercises.explanation: रोटी is feminine and has no postposition, so the verb agrees with it: खाई (feminine singular past of खाना).

exercises.prompt: Fill in: "मैंने गाने _____ ।" (I sang songs.) — गाने is masculine plural.
exercises.explanation: गाने (songs) is masculine plural with no postposition, so the verb agrees: गाए (masculine plural past of गाना). गाई would be feminine singular, गाया masculine singular.

sections.title: Ergative vs. Non-Ergative Contrast
sections.explanation:
Understanding the ergative system requires seeing the contrast between perfective (with ने) and imperfective (without ने) constructions side by side.

IMPERFECTIVE (no ने, verb agrees with subject):
  वह किताब पढ़ता है। = He reads a book. (पढ़ता agrees with वह, masculine subject)
  वह किताब पढ़ती है। = She reads a book. (पढ़ती agrees with वह, feminine subject)

PERFECTIVE (ने, verb agrees with object):
  उसने किताब पढ़ी। = He read a book. (पढ़ी agrees with किताब, feminine object)
  उसने किताब पढ़ी। = She read a book. (पढ़ी agrees with किताब — same form regardless of subject gender!)

Notice that in the perfective, whether the subject is male or female does not matter — the verb form is identical because it agrees with the object.

SPECIAL VERBS:
Some transitive verbs like लाना (to bring) and भूलना (to forget) can be used with or without ने depending on dialect and context:
  मैं भूल गया / मैंने भूल गया — both are heard.

COMPOUND VERBS:
In compound verbs (verb + auxiliary like लेना, देना, जाना), ने usage depends on the auxiliary verb:
  उसने खा लिया। = He ate up. (लेना is transitive → ने used)
  वह खा गया। = He ate. (जाना is intransitive → no ने)
sections.tip: The simplest test: say the same sentence in present habitual and simple past. If the verb form changes to match the object in the past, you know ने is at work.

examples.hi: वह फल खाता है। → उसने फल खाया।
examples.en: He eats fruit. → He ate fruit. (imperfective → perfective with ने)

examples.hi: वह गाना गाती है। → उसने गाना गाया।
examples.en: She sings a song. → She sang a song. (verb agrees with गाना masculine in perfective)

examples.hi: बच्चे दूध पीते हैं। → बच्चों ने दूध पिया।
examples.en: Children drink milk. → Children drank milk.

examples.hi: उसने खाना खा लिया। (compound verb with लेना → ने used)
examples.en: He ate up the food. (लेना is transitive, so ने is required)

examples.hi: वह चला गया। (compound verb with जाना → no ने)
examples.en: He went away. (जाना is intransitive, so no ने)

exercises.prompt: Convert to perfective: "वह चाय पीती है" (She drinks tea.) What is the correct past tense?
exercises.explanation: पीना is transitive, so the perfective requires ने: उसने. चाय is feminine, so the verb is पी (feminine singular past). पिया would be masculine, which is incorrect for चाय.

exercises.prompt: बच्चों ___ दूध पिया। (The children drank milk.)
exercises.hint: Postposition for transitive verbs in perfective
exercises.explanation: पीना is transitive and the action is perfective, so the subject takes ने. दूध is masculine, so the verb is पिया (masculine singular).

exercises.prompt: "उसने खाना खा लिया" uses ने, but "वह चला गया" does not. Why?
options: Because खाना is a longer word than चलना
Because in compound verbs, ने depends on the auxiliary: लेना is transitive (ने), जाना is intransitive (no ने)
Because खा लिया is past tense and चला गया is present tense
exercises.explanation: In compound verbs, the auxiliary verb determines ने usage. लेना (to take) is transitive, so ने is used. जाना (to go) is intransitive, so ने is not used. Both sentences are in the perfective past tense.
